title: The Out-of-Vocabulary Problem
concept: When a model encounters a word or character not in its vocabulary table, it fails; subword tokenization minimizes this but doesn't eliminate it completely.

exercises.prompt: In Natural Language Processing, what does the acronym OOV stand for?
exercises.explain: The abbreviation OOV stands for 'Out-of-Vocabulary', which describes words that a model cannot recognize because they aren't in its pre-defined dictionary.

cards.title: When AI Gets Tongue-Tied
cards.body: Have you ever read a book in a foreign language and stumbled upon a word you've never seen before? You get stuck because that word isn't in your personal mental dictionary. AI models face the exact same struggle!

cards.title: What is the OOV Problem?
cards.body: The Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) problem happens when an AI model processes text containing words, symbols, or slang that are not in its pre-made vocabulary list. When this happens, the model usually has to replace the unknown word with a generic placeholder like '<UNK>' (Unknown), losing its meaning.

cards.title: The Tiny Dictionary Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a dictionary that only has room for 1,000 common English words. If someone asks you to look up 'supercalifragilistic', you won't find it! The word is 'out-of-vocabulary' because it is simply not in your dictionary's limited pages, leaving you unable to translate or understand it.

cards.title: Lost in Translation
cards.body: If a model's vocabulary list only includes common words like 'run', 'fast', and 'dog', and a user types 'doggo', the model won't recognize it. It will convert 'doggo' into '<UNK>', seeing the sentence 'I love my doggo' as 'I love my <UNK>'.

cards.title: Are Subwords a Perfect Cure?
cards.body: Many believe that modern 'subword tokenizers' (which break words into smaller chunks like 'play' and 'ing') completely cure the OOV problem. While they help a lot by building new words from smaller pieces, they still fail when they hit brand new emojis, foreign alphabets, or completely novel slang characters!

cards.title: Known vs. Unknown Words
cards.body: Compare how an AI model handles words it knows versus words that are completely missing from its vocabulary database.

right.text: The word 'skibidi' is missing, so the AI replaces it with '<UNK>' and loses all context.

left.text: The word 'happiness' is found in the dictionary, so the AI understands its full meaning perfectly.

exercises.prompt: How do subword tokenizers help minimize the Out-of-Vocabulary problem?

options.text: They completely eliminate all unknown words in every language.
They reduce the problem by breaking words into smaller, familiar chunks.
They increase the vocabulary size to infinity so nothing is missed.
They prevent users from typing slang words.

exercises.explain: Subword tokenizers split rare words into smaller known parts (like 'un' + 'believable'), which helps reduce unknown words, but they still cannot handle totally unknown unique characters or symbols.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Modern subword tokenization has completely eliminated the Out-of-Vocabulary problem for all text.
exercises.explain: Even the best modern tokenizers can run into OOV issues when faced with entirely new symbols, emojis, or characters from other alphabets that weren't in their training data.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to describe where a model looks up its known words:
exercises.sentence: A model fails to recognize a word when it is missing from its lookup table, known as the vocabulary.
exercises.explain: If a word is not present in the model's vocabulary table, it cannot be recognized or processed correctly, resulting in the OOV problem.

exercises.prompt:
Two AIs encounter the slang word 'doggo'. 
System A outputs: 'I love my <UNK>'
System B outputs: 'I love my dog-go'
Which system handled the OOV problem better?

options.text: System A is smarter because it flags spelling errors with <UNK>.
System B is better because it reconstructs the word using subword pieces.

exercises.explain: System B successfully processes the sentence because 'dog' + 'go' are known subwords, whereas System A completely gives up on the word 'doggo' and replaces it with the unknown token <UNK>.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a chatbot for a medical website, and it keeps failing when users type in rare disease names like 'fibrodysplasia'. Which of the following best describes this system failure?

options.text: A bug where a model runs out of computer memory when loading too many documents.
A failure to process a word because it was never included in the model's official vocabulary list.
A security error that occurs when a user types an incorrect password into a secure database.
An error where the model mixes up English words with foreign words during translation.

exercises.explain: The Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) problem occurs when a text contains a word that was not present in the model's training vocabulary, causing it to fail to understand or process that word correctly.
